parsing_res_list.block_content: Canadian exports to U.S. rose in November
Canada-U.s. merchandise trade Monthlyimporandexos,inillionsofollars
THE GLOBE AND MAIL, SOURCE: STATISTICS CANADA
uplus my hve boliticalsignificance amid ru's complaints about he trade deficit
MARKRENDELL

anada posd is nin-coeive trade deficit in November,ut its trade surplus with the nited States grew that month,ighlighting a key economic dyamic that has political implicaions ahead of Donald Trump's eturn to the White House later hismonth.

Canada's total merchandise rade exports increased 2.2 per ent in November, while imports creased 1.8 per cent, narrowing hecountry's tradedeficitwith he rest of the world to $3-mion from a downwardly revised 544-million the month before,tatistics Canada reported Tuesav.
At the same time, the longtanding goods trade surplus iththe United States increased $8.2-billion from $6.6-billion in ctober, with exports to the U.S.p 6.8 per cent month-to-month
That monthly trade surplus –neresult of Canadaexporting e o t d a h nports – is on the small side,ompared to the average in re
cent years. However, it may carry larger political significance than usual, given Mr. Trump's complaints about the U.S. trade deficit with Canada and his threats of tariff s.

The president-elect has promised to slap 25-per-cent tariffs on Canadian imports if Ottawa does not address American concerns about border security. Mr. Trump has also complained about his country's merchandise trade deficit with Canada, which has averaged a little over $1oo-billion annually for the passt three years on around $1.1-trillion in annual two-way trade. The trade deficit is
smaller when services are taken into account.

"We lose in trade deficits. We're losing massive," Mr. Trump said of trade with Canada in a news conference on Tuesday. "You know, they make 2o per cent of our cars. We don't need that. I'd rather make them in Detroit. We don't need their cars. We don't need their lumber. …. We don't need their dairy products. We have more than they have. We don't need anything."
Ta omy relies heavily on access to the U.S. market. It's the destination for around 75 per cent of Can

adian exports, including $166-billion worth of oil and gas exports and $85-billion worth of vehicle and auto parts exports in 2023.High and broad-based tariffs could push Canada into a recession and stunt business investment.

"The outlook for Canadian trade in 2025 is cloudy at best,"Toronto-Dominion Bank economist Marc Ercolao wrote in a note to clients. "Our base case is that Canada largely avoids a full-scale implementation of Trump's tariff plan, given our energy-heavy relationship with the U.S. – though we acknowledge that any tariff  levied against Canada has negative consequences for economic growth."

The increase in overall Canadian exports was broad-based in November, with metals and nonmetallic ores up 10.5 per cent, industrial machinery and parts up 3.8 per cent and aircraft and transportation equipment up 6.7 per cent. Energy exports rose 2.2 per cent, with a 4.8-per-cent jump in crude oil exports on stronger prices.
Motor-vehicle exports, by contrast, contracted 1.2 per cent in November and remain considerably lower than a year ago.
a Canada's exports to the U.S.have been helped in recent months by a depreciation in the Canadian dollar against the U.S.dollar, which makes Canadian products more appealing to American buyers.

Exports may also be getting a boost as companies try to "front run" potential tariffs by moving goods across the border pre-emp tively. A survey of more than 300Canadian companies in Decem ber, conducted by the organiza tion Canadian Manufacturers &Exporters, found that about one third are accelerating shipments to the U.S.
公司添
In volume terms, total exports were up 0.5 per cent in November

a export volumes in November provides further evidence that the economy was gaining mo mentum at the end of last year,"Stephen Brown, deputy chief North America economist at Capital Economics, wrote in a note to clients. "U.S. tariffs could cause the recovery to go into reverse this year, but that threat might provide some further support in the near term if U.S. importers try to front-run the tariffs."

Merchandise exports to countries other than  the U.S. decreased 10.3 per cent in November, driven by lower shipments of gold to Hong Kong and nickel to Norway. Canada's trade deficit with countries other than the United States widened to $8.5-billion in November from $7.2-billion in October.
Service exports, meanwhile,contracted o.2 per cent, leaving the services trade deficit at about $300-million,  essentially  unchanged from the previous month.

to's St. Michael's Hospital.

"John was a remarkable person in so many ways," said Melissa Martin, the chair of the St. Michael's Hospital Foundation. "He had an extraordinary business career, but beyond that he was a magnificent fundraiser."

In 2011, Mr. Hunkin played a key role in securing a $25-million donation to St. Michael's from Hong Kong billionaire Li Kashing, who had a significant investment in CIBC. Ms. Martin said the donation "ignited the hospital's research initiatives and elevated our scientists' work to new heights."
E Mr. Hunkin also supported Toronto's Centre for Addiction and Mental Health and the Montreal Museum of Fine Arts Foundation.In 2019, Governor-General Julie Payette awarded him the Order of Canada.

Early in his banking career, he moved to London to help open CIBC's European credit division,new territory for the bank. The pioneering role caught the eye of CIBC executive Don Fullerton,who subsequently became CEO.He tasked Mr. Hunkin and colleague Al Flood with setting a strategy for U.S. expansion. The two bankers moved to New York and built a successful corporate lending team, run by Mr. Flood,who went on to succeed Mr. Fullerton as the bank's CEO and Mr.Hunkin's predecessor

a government liberalized financial regulations, allowing banks to own investment dealers. CiBC acquired Wood Gundy after the dealer lost much of its capital in the 1987 market crash. Initially,the two cultures clashed

"John was the first banker to cross the line, to turn his back on his career as a corporate banker and jump into investment dealer,and he did it well, said David Kassie, head of investment banking at CIBC and former chair and CEO at Canaccord Genuity Group Inc. "John excelled at building client relationships and was a really good manager of people."

"Putting an investment dealer and a bank together was uncharted territory, but John made it happen, mainly because everyone trusted him," said Tom Woods,CIBC's former chief financial officer. "He was not afraid to take risks. CIBC's electronic banking initiative in the U.S. was years ahead of its time, but ultimately had to be wound up."
公司名CEO, and the CIBC board faced a choice between Mr. Hunkin and head of retail banking Holger Kluge as his successor. When Mr.Hunkin got the nod, Mr. Kluge departed.

CIBC became a leader in wealth management by acquiring the retail brokerage network of Merrill Lynch Canada Inc. for $6oo-million.
years of his banking career dealing with Enron litigation. His successor, Gerry McCaughey, eventually settled the lawsuits for $2.5billion.

Mr. Hunkin retired in 2005, and he and his wife, Susan Crocker, a former CIBC executive and CEO of the Healthcare of Ontario Pension Plan, moved to Chester, N.S.That summer, he skippered his newly acquired 48-foot yacht in the biennial Marblehead to Halifax Ocean Race, a 36o-mile event from Massachusetts to Nova Scotia. He pledged to lease an intrpretation r outlining the rules governing nurse practitioners and other non-physician health care is a t  eda Halth t,which was passed in 1984 and only explicitly covers services from physicians and in hospitals.

a In the intervening years, nurse practitioners, pharmacists and other health professionals have been granted new authorities to order tests, prescribe medications and provide other services that would otherwise come from a primary-care physician. But they have been left in a grey area that allows them to operate as both public and private providers in a way physicians are barred from.
Mark Holland, the Health Minister since July, 2023, has continued to promise legal clarity, but a letter has so far not been released.
Whenever we have providers that are using out-of-pocket,or private pay options, they are being pulled out of the public system to provide that service.
